decision: Treat Linux with KVM as the only native runtime host for Firecracker and document macOS/Windows as operator paths through a Linux/KVM guest or remote runner.
rationale: Firecracker requires the Linux KVM module and read/write access to /dev/kvm; the current host is macOS arm64 without /dev/kvm, so native macOS execution cannot be claimed.
alternatives: Claim native macOS/Windows support was rejected because it is not supported by the Firecracker runtime requirement; adding a second hypervisor backend in this PR was rejected as an architectural expansion beyond the current Firecracker daemon boundary.

decision: Materialize writable root filesystems as per-VM disk copies under daemon storage instead of writing directly to the registered image rootfs.
rationale: A shared writable image rootfs leaks filesystem state across VMs and violates both persistence isolation and repeatable image behavior; per-VM copies preserve session state while keeping source images immutable.
alternatives: Read-only rootfs was rejected because the objective requires persistent filesystem behavior; snapshot-only persistence was rejected because snapshot creation is currently stubbed and cannot satisfy the current closed-loop objective.

decision: Implement the first egress enforcement slice as per-VM L3/L4 iptables policy with proxy-only support rather than embedding an L7 credential-injecting proxy in nanofuse.
rationale: The repo already documents a standalone daax-egress proxy model; nanofuse should enforce forced routing and fail-closed packet policy while leaving L7 parsing and credential injection to the proxy boundary.
alternatives: Embedding an L7 proxy library in nanofused was rejected because it duplicates the planned sidecar and expands credential-handling risk; documentation-only egress was rejected because the objective requires a testable enforcement path.

decision: Treat macOS arm64 with the Parallels Vagrant provider as a capability preflight environment only unless the guest exposes /dev/kvm.
rationale: The local closed-loop run booted the Ubuntu 24.04 arm64 guest and synced the repo, then failed provisioning at the required /dev/kvm check. Running guest-side Firecracker, daemon health, or kernel/rootfs verification without KVM would produce a false validation claim.
alternatives: Skipping the KVM preflight was rejected because Firecracker cannot provide the intended microVM security boundary without KVM; marking Parallels validation as successful was rejected because the Firecracker runtime path was not exercised.

decision: Make Parallels nested virtualization an explicit opt-in Vagrant setting instead of enabling it unconditionally.
rationale: The local VM accepted --nested-virt on but then failed at prlctl start, so forcing the setting would prevent even capability preflight on this host. Default-off plus NANOFUSE_PARALLELS_NESTED=1 preserves a test path for providers that support it while keeping the default harness bootable.
alternatives: Always enabling nested virtualization was rejected because it made the local Parallels guest unbootable; silently leaving a manually toggled VM setting was rejected because Vagrant runs would become state-dependent.

decision: Expose runtime readiness through a first-party GET /capabilities API endpoint and document Mac/Windows clients as remote API clients of a Linux/KVM daemon.
rationale: Health alone only proves the daemon can answer requests; clients and tray apps need a stable API response that distinguishes reachable control plane from local Firecracker runtime capability.
alternatives: Relying on documentation-only platform checks was rejected because clients could not gate actions programmatically; adding native macOS/Windows runtime backends was rejected as an unapproved architecture change and incompatible with the current Firecracker/KVM boundary.

decision: Capture the macOS and Windows tray/menu app as an API-only client requirement and defer desktop framework selection.
rationale: Choosing Electron, Tauri, Wails, Swift, or WinUI would add repository-wide dependencies and packaging architecture. The immediate requirement is to preserve the daemon boundary and define the API interactions the UI must use.
alternatives: Adding a desktop runtime in this PR was rejected because it is a cross-stack architectural decision; a daemon-bypassing native UI was rejected because it would violate the API/client boundary documented in .claude/architecture.md.

decision: Compare Nanofuse against E2B, Daytona, Modal, Fly Machines, Docker Engine, and Firecracker as distinct API layers rather than treating all sandbox tools as equivalent.
rationale: The tools expose different abstractions: process/file/code SDKs, hosted machine lifecycle APIs, container daemon APIs, and raw VMM APIs. Nanofuse currently sits at the self-hosted microVM control-plane layer and needs a future guest interaction API for parity with code-execution platforms.
alternatives: A generic comparison was rejected because it would hide important API gaps; claiming parity with E2B, Daytona, or Modal was rejected because Nanofuse does not yet expose in-VM command/file/code execution APIs.

decision: Keep VM deletion database semantics warn-and-continue when storage cleanup fails.
rationale: Delete already treats Firecracker stop, SPIRE cleanup, port forwards, TAP deletion, IPAM release, and egress cleanup as best-effort operations. Failing the API delete after partial runtime cleanup would strand the database row and make repeated deletes depend on filesystem cleanup state.
alternatives: Returning HTTP 500 on VM storage cleanup failure was rejected because it changed established delete semantics and was flagged in PR review; skipping storage cleanup entirely was rejected because writable VM rootfs state must be removed when possible.

decision: Generate egress iptables chain names from a deterministic SHA-256 prefix of the full VM ID.
rationale: The previous sanitized and truncated VM ID prefix could collide for distinct IDs sharing the first characters. A 16-hex-character digest prefix keeps the NF-EG- chain under the 29-character iptables limit while depending on the full VM ID.
alternatives: Increasing the readable prefix length was rejected because it still allows prefix collisions; using the raw VM ID was rejected because UUID separators and long IDs can exceed iptables chain-name constraints.

decision: Fold active Dependabot Go module and GitHub Actions version bumps into codex-goal, but close the Ubuntu 26.04 base-image bump as not relevant to this branch.
rationale: The module and action bumps are maintenance updates that can be validated with the branch CI. The Ubuntu 26.04 PR changes the platform baseline while Nanofuse requirements, Vagrant setup, base image manifests, Dockerfiles, and documentation explicitly target Ubuntu 24.04 LTS.
alternatives: Leaving all Dependabot PRs open was rejected because the operator asked to squash relevant active items into this branch; folding the Ubuntu 26.04 bump was rejected because it would require a separate base-image compatibility decision and documentation sweep.

decision: Migrate CLI table rendering to the tablewriter v1 API while preserving compact borderless output.
rationale: The active Dependabot update from tablewriter 0.0.5 to 1.1.4 removes the legacy SetHeader and SetAlignment methods. Adapting the formatter keeps the dependency current without dropping tabular CLI output.
alternatives: Excluding the tablewriter update was rejected because the API break is small and fixable; replacing tablewriter with handwritten column formatting was rejected because it would add local formatting logic for a dependency already in use.

decision: Add network.setup daemon config to allow explicit no-network rootless KVM validation
rationale: Linux hosts with read/write /dev/kvm can boot Firecracker as an unprivileged kvm-group user, but nanofused previously always configured bridge and NAT at startup, requiring root even for VMs requested with network.mode=none. The explicit opt-out preserves the default privileged production behavior while enabling Mac-to-Linux API validation without sudo.
alternatives: Keeping unconditional bridge/NAT setup was rejected because it prevents no-network API validation. Making rootless mode implicit was rejected because it could silently disable networking in production.

decision: Do not treat the local Parallels VM on this Apple M2 Max as a supported Firecracker runtime target.
rationale: Current local tests show the guest has no /dev/kvm, Apple Virtualization reports nested virtualization unsupported, and Parallels fails to start the VM when nested virtualization is enabled. Firecracker requires read/write /dev/kvm, so accepting this path would be a false closed-loop result.
alternatives: Continuing to retry Parallels on this host was rejected because the provider cannot expose the required KVM device; using remote Linux/KVM for runtime remains valid but does not satisfy local Parallels acceptance.

decision: Make CLI API connection errors report the selected endpoint instead of falling back to the default Unix socket.
rationale: Mac and Windows launch flows primarily use NANOFUSE_API_URL or --api-url, so a TCP failure that names /run/nanofused.sock sends operators to the wrong remediation path. Endpoint-aware errors keep local socket and remote/tunneled API debugging separate.
alternatives: Keeping the socket-only fallback was rejected because it hides the actual failing URL; printing both socket and URL was rejected because only one transport is active for a given client invocation.

decision: Return INVALID_CONFIG for managed-network VM creates when daemon network.setup is disabled and keep Cobra usage enabled for ordinary CLI errors.
rationale: A requested NAT network cannot be satisfied by a daemon explicitly configured without network setup, so the API must report a client-visible configuration mismatch instead of an internal server error. The CLI should suppress Cobra output only for already formatted Nanofuse errors so unknown commands, argument mistakes, and flag parsing still show Cobra diagnostics and usage.
alternatives: Keeping HTTP 500 was rejected because the failure is deterministic configuration mismatch. Keeping global Cobra SilenceUsage and SilenceErrors was rejected because it hides standard command usage for non-API errors.

decision: Use daax-dev/vagrant-skill as the required local Vagrant harness for this branch and keep dev/vagrant as a secondary repo-local harness.
rationale: The operator explicitly required vagrant-skill in objective.md. The prior validation used the repo-local dev/vagrant harness first, which did not satisfy that requirement even though it exercised similar provider checks.
alternatives: Continuing with only dev/vagrant was rejected because it ignores the stated harness requirement; editing the external vagrant-skill repository was rejected because the operator restricted work to this repo and branch.

decision: Implement the tray/menu app now as a minimal Go API client using getlantern/systray for macOS and Windows.
rationale: The operator requires a runnable tray/menu UI on the current branch. A small Go systray entry point preserves the existing Go project shape, keeps all VM operations behind nanofused, and avoids introducing a full webview or Electron/Tauri/Wails toolchain before the API client behavior is proven.
alternatives: Documentation-only tray requirements were rejected because the operator asked for a running app; Electron, Tauri, Wails, native Swift, and WinUI were rejected for this branch because they add larger packaging decisions or multiple codebases before the API boundary is validated.

decision: Make the tray app's primary launch path select a cached image and create/start a VM through nanofused.
rationale: The operator clarified the Mac tray requirement as launching VMs based on containers and named SlicerVM as the product guide. Nanofuse already represents container-derived runtime artifacts as cached images, so the minimal correct tray workflow is select image, POST /vms, then POST /vms/{id}/start while keeping direct hypervisor control out of the desktop app.
alternatives: Leaving the tray app as existing-VM-only management was rejected because it does not launch container-derived VMs. Adding arbitrary text entry or full image pull progress in the menu was deferred because systray menus do not provide a reliable native text input path and image pull progress requires a larger UI slice.

decision: Make the macOS tray launcher observable and non-silent by default.
rationale: The previous script execed the tray binary with no operator-visible status after a successful build, so an already running or background menu bar app could look like no action happened. Printing build/start status, existing PIDs, log path, and restart guidance makes the command result explicit while preserving the same API-only tray app.
alternatives: Keeping the foreground exec behavior was rejected because it provides no launch confirmation. Always killing an existing tray was rejected because replacing a desktop process should require explicit --restart. Building a packaged macOS .app bundle was deferred because the immediate defect is launcher observability, not distribution packaging.

decision: Add a macOS-native runtime backend that launches container-backed Linux microVMs through Apple's container CLI and Virtualization.framework instead of requiring Linux KVM on macOS.
rationale: The operator explicitly changed the requirement from Firecracker-only to working local macOS microVMs. Apple documents container as running Linux containers in lightweight VMs on Apple silicon, and local validation showed container 0.4.1 can start an Alpine Linux guest on this macOS 26.5 arm64 host without /dev/kvm.
alternatives: Continuing to require a Linux/KVM VM on macOS was rejected because it fails the local Mac product requirement. Building a direct Swift Virtualization.framework helper first was deferred because Apple's installed container runtime already provides a supported container-to-lightweight-VM path on this host and lets Nanofuse wire the API/tray loop immediately.

decision: Align repo Vagrant/release documentation with the Go 1.25 module target and run vagrant-skill from a fresh Vagrant state directory after the prior Parallels state became invalid.
rationale: The current go.mod requires Go 1.25.0, while the repo-local Vagrant setup and release docs still referenced Go 1.24. The fresh vagrant-skill VM initially installed Go 1.24.3 and could not run mage. The existing vagrant-skill state also pointed at a missing Parallels .pvm file, so a new VAGRANT_DOTFILE_PATH and VM name were used to validate this branch without editing the external vagrant-skill repository or relying on broken local state.
alternatives: Downgrading the module target was rejected because CI already pins Go 1.25. Editing the external vagrant-skill repository was rejected because this task is constrained to the nanofuse branch. Skipping Vagrant was rejected because the operator requires vagrant-skill validation.

decision: Launch the macOS tray app through the per-user launchd GUI domain instead of direct shell backgrounding.
rationale: Closed-loop testing showed the raw background process could exit after the launcher shell ended, while foreground execution and launchd GUI-domain execution stayed alive and reached the API. Using launchd preserves logs, restart behavior, and a stable stop command without requiring a packaged app bundle in this branch.
alternatives: Plain background execution with '&' and nohup was rejected because the process still exited after startup. Building a full .app bundle was deferred because launchd submit fixes the runnable tray requirement while preserving the existing script-based developer workflow.

decision: Gate macOS Apple-container runtime readiness on a direct host virtualization probe and skip pre-delete kill when a stale VM row has no runtime handle.
rationale: The Apple container CLI being installed does not prove the host can execute Virtualization.framework-backed VMs, so /capabilities now probes kern.hv_support and uses that result for both virtualization_framework_supported and native runtime readiness. A stale Running or Paused VM row without PID or external runtime ID cannot be killed, but runtime Delete can still clean resources by deterministic VM ID on Apple-container and Firecracker Delete remains metadata-safe.
alternatives: Continuing to infer Virtualization.framework support from the container binary was rejected because it can advertise a runtime that will fail later. Returning HTTP 500 before runtime Delete for handle-less stale rows was rejected because it makes those rows undeletable.

decision: Fail fast when runtime.driver is incompatible with the daemon host OS.
rationale: A daemon configured for firecracker on macOS or apple_container on non-macOS hosts cannot satisfy its runtime contract and will fail later during startup or VM lifecycle operations. Config validation now resolves auto by host OS and rejects explicit incompatible drivers with actionable errors.
alternatives: Allowing incompatible explicit drivers was rejected because it converts a deterministic configuration error into later runtime failures. Silently falling back to the host default was rejected because it ignores the operator's explicit runtime selection.
